S 
et-jetting. Have you come across this expression? The trend of 
traveling inspired by films and series has earned a playful name: 
set-jetting (a clever play on "jet-setting" and "filming set"). Both current 
hits and pop-culture classics fuel the desire to discover destinations 
portrayed on screen, turning hotels, restaurants and monuments into 
hotspots. The phenomenon may not be new, but it has seen a major 
resurgence in recent years. Clips from productions go viral online and 
shape travelers' wishlists. The algorithm feeds the enthusiasm: as 
streaming adoption grows, more and more people are affected (by 
2029, there will be 2 billion streaming subscriptions worldwide, according 
to Ampere Analysis).
8:5:
TRAVEL INSPIRED  
BY FILMS AND SERIES
8:6:
• THE WHITE LOTUS EFFECT
The White Lotus — a satire on the dynamic between guests and staff 
at the fictional White Lotus resorts — has become one of HBO's biggest 
titles in the streaming era. And that cult following reverberates across 
tourism. Take the most recent batch of episodes, shot in Thailand. The 
Thai government forecasted a record number of visits in 2025: 40 
million tourists, many of them drawn in by the show. Four Seasons is 
the production's main partner; it's their hotels that bring most of the 
properties to life (with some poetic license). Four Seasons Resort Koh 
Samui hosted a large share of the third-season filming, including 
common areas and villas. You'll easily recognize the Three-Bedroom 
Family Residence Villa, spanning 1,130 m², as the accommodation 
of magnate Timothy Ratliff (Jason Isaacs) and his family. The result: 
online searches for the resort spiked 40% on booking sites. It wasn't 
the first time. In the second season, set in Sicily, The White Lotus drove 
a 300% surge in search for stays at San Domenico Palace, Taormina, 
a Four Seasons Hotel — the hotel went six months without being able 
to accept new bookings.
8:9:
NOT EVERYTHING IS  
WHAT YOU SEE
1. 
The suite booked by the Mossbacher family in the first 
season is one of the two presidential accommodations at 
Four Seasons Resort Maui at Wailea. With three bedrooms, 
it can house six adults and five children across 372 m². 
So no one has to sleep on the living room sofa or the 
kitchen floor, as siblings Olivia (Sydney Sweeney) and Quinn 
(Fred Hechinger) do.
2. 
The star of the second season, San Domenico Palace, 
appears on screen as a seaside resort, but the reality 
is different. The hotel sits atop a hill in Taormina, with views 
over the Ionian Sea and Mount Etna. No toes in the sand: 
there's no private beach, just a beach service reached by 
car. It was another case of "television magic." The beach 
scenes were filmed in Cefalù, a coastal town about two 
hours away.
3. 
In the third season, the wellness center with lotus-flower 
ponds actually belongs to another hotel: Anantara 
Mai Khao, located in Phuket.

One in ten tourists felt compelled to 
travel to Paris after watching films and 
series set in the city; of those, more than

• GEEK CINEMATOGRAPHY
Aired between 2011 and 2019, Game of Thrones 
— another HBO megaproduction — significantly 
changed the dynamics of travel to Croatia. 
It's estimated that, in the five years after the 
show's premiere, the Eastern European country 
received more than € 180 million from tourism 
related to the "medieval fantasy tale." In that 
same period, more than 240,000 tourists are 
believed to have landed in Dubrovnik exclusively 
because of Game of Thrones. The walled city 
played an important role in filming, chosen as 
the face of King's Landing — the capital of the 
realms of Westeros. Shooting took place in 
several other Croatian destinations, notably 
Diocletian's Palace and Klis Fortress (whose 
annual visits jumped 900%!), both in Split, which 
stood in for the slave city of Meereen, freed by 
Daenerys Targaryen played by Emilia Clarke.
 
Long before Game of Thrones, other fantasy 
sagas encouraged travelers to pack their bags. 
The success of The Lord of the Rings trilogy, in 
the early 2000s, led pop-culture enthusiasts to 
seek itineraries through New Zealand (there's 
one on p. 12!). The country's unbelievable 
natural landscapes — from pristine beaches 
to mountains with "eternal" snow — gave 
structure to the mythical Middle-earth 
universe of writer J. R. R. Tolkien. Part of the built 
scenography was preserved, making it possible 
to visit Hobbiton; the rural area on the outskirts 
of Waikato, on the North Island, was permanently 
transformed into the Shire, the hobbits' village. 
There, you can see and explore the little houses 
hidden beneath the hills, including the one 
belonging to the protagonist Frodo (Elijah Wood). 
To this day, set visits contribute billions of dollars 
to New Zealand's economy.
10:2:
Star Wars, the intergalactic journey of director 
George Lucas, transformed the most remote 
and desolate locations in southern Tunisia into
10:3:
places of pilgrimage for devotees of the 
Jedi. On the big screen, the land takes the 
name Tatooine, where the villain Darth 
Vader lived as a child. Near the town of 
Tataouine (now you know where the 
fictional planet's name comes from), old 
granaries carved in stone by Berber peoples 
around the 11th century were used as the 
slave quarter, where young Anakin 
Skywalker lived in the infamous Episode I 
– The Phantom Menace. In the Tozeur oasis, 
the Mos Espa spaceport still stands with 
abandoned props — and has even hosted 
a rave for fans.
10:4:
In the same vein of intergalactic sagas, 
it's worth mentioning Denis Villeneuve's 
epic Dune. In the current retelling of 
this classic (the book was published in 
1965 and later adapted to the big screen 
in 1984 by David Lynch), two destinations 
were combined to shape the arid planet 
Arrakis. The scenes featuring dunes 
stretching as far as the eye can see were 
captured in the Abu Dhabi desert, while 
those with imposing rock formations are 
from the Wadi Rum desert, in Jordan.
10:5:
The country was also the setting for the 
Indiana Jones franchise which, even 
though it only touches on "intergalactic" 
themes in Indiana Jones and the 
Kingdom of the Crystal Skull (2008), is 
always geeky at heart, with its 
protagonist passionate about 
archaeology, history and ancient 
civilizations. In Indiana Jones and the 
Last Crusade (1989), the temple of Al 
Khazneh, known as The Treasury (read 
about it on p. 95), appears in the film 
housing the mythical Holy Grail. That 
appearance was responsible for placing 
the stunning building — carved into the 
reddish stone of Petra — not only into 
pop culture, but also on the radar of 
many travelers.

stables into a festive pop-up store. Among 
the seasonal products will be cheese biscuits, Christmas 
puddings and the gins produced by the Royal Collection Trust, 
along with collectible ceramics, ornaments and plush toys. 
Open from November 14 to January 5.

You've just opened  
Bonaccia Osteria in Petrópolis.  
What memories, or influences,  
did you draw on to build the menu?
29:9:
Bonaccia grew out of a long-held dream to pay tribute 
to my family's Italian past. We created 
an Italian cuisine without borders, trying to cover 
various regions and traditions, with a touch of freshness and 
local products. A relaxed kitchen, with 
excellent-quality ingredients and an authorial touch.
29:10:
We know you're not particularly  
fond of champagne in your cuisine...  
So what can't be missing?
29:11:
It's not that I'm not fond of champagne — 
I love a good champagne. But it's nowhere near 
my favorite drink. What I think can't be missing 
is good food! A light atmosphere, hospitality at its 
maximum and few protocols.
 
Lasai's recipes “speak” 
about sustainability and respect  
for local ingredients. When did that 
become a priority?
29:12:
It has been one of Lasai's priorities since the first 
day we opened the doors. I learned a lot about it 
when I lived five years in the Basque Country (San Sebastián). 
For me, it was a school in how to treat products and 
how to respect local suppliers. We've had our own garden 
even before opening Lasai. For us it was a natural 
movement to always prioritize our own products and local 
organics — without waving the flag too much.
29:13:
Of all the dishes you've  
created, which is your favorite?
29:14:
What I actually love most is the sequence of 
appetizers we always serve at the start of the menu.
 
Is there a place you still 
haven't been to, but that you believe has the 
potential to sharpen your culinary 
creativity when you visit?
29:15:
My dream is to go to Japan — can you believe I've never been? 
I'd love to spend about 30 days in the country exploring 
the many culinary traditions found there. I'm a true 
lover of Japanese food — whenever I go to 
São Paulo, my first stops are the 
excellent Japanese restaurants of the city.
 
If you could choose only one:  
which restaurant in the world justifies  
a trip just to visit it? And why?

Etxebarri (p. 65), in the Basque Country. Everything done over firewood, 
spectacular local products, plenty of vegetables on the 
embers. And always finishing with a splendid piece of meat on the 
grill with just flour de sel — unbelievably good.

he place to be! The opening of the first Brazilian W hotel 
last year also brought to São Paulo a new gastronomic 
hotspot: Baio Cozinha Sulista. At the restaurant, with a menu 
signed by Tuca Mezzomo, flavors of gaucho heritage shine. 
A native of Passo Fundo, the chef defines southern cuisine as 
“rich, intense and full of memory”, influenced by Italian, German, 
Portuguese and Spanish migrations. The menu mirrors this 
diversity in dishes such as the purple sweet potato gnocchi, 
served with goat cheese fonduta and walnut praline, delivering 
just the right intensity on the palate. Fire-based cooking 
techniques are house highlights: cuts of meat go into the Josper 
— a hybrid grill and oven — and are paired with inventive sauces; 
yerba mate béarnaise is among the options. The chimarrão 
mousse with pistachio and mint can close the meal with a 
refreshing note, while you take in, from a privileged vantage, 
the urban skyline — the restaurant occupies the 23rd floor of 
the hotel. On Sundays, from 1 pm to 5 pm, a lively brunch is 
served with DJ sets and a gin-tonic bar. A polished program, 
and unmissable.

INY, delicate. Teardrop peas 
are among the most seasonal vegetables 
(on the menu only in April) and the most 
precious (up to 800 euros per 
kilo). They must be picked at dusk, and only growers 
in the Basque Country have mastered the technique. When they 
arrived at my table, accompanied by ecological 
Riofrio Osetra caviar and shrimp tartare, just 
24 hours had passed since the harvest.
30:18:
The combination is exquisitely flavorful, and the sensation of the tiny 
drops nearly “exploding” on the tongue is something that stays 
in the memory. The pairing with caviar is especially playful, 
since the peas are nicknamed green caviar. 
It is this level of preciousness that earned chef Jurgen van der 
Zalm two Michelin stars at Vinkeles, inside the 
well-located, charming, boutique hotel The Dylan.
Another tartare, of sea bass, is a signature dish, served 
on a crisp, sweet and airy meringue made with cucumber 
juice. An amuse-bouche of sea bream and tzatziki sorbet 
(Greek cucumber and yogurt paste) came with wild garlic 
flower. The potato foam — soft, soft. The smoked 
eel? It won my heart. Thus, dinner begins 
delicate and refreshing before navigating maritime 
flavors full of personality, passing through langoustines 
(always present in the chef's creations, drawing much 
from the sea) to arrive at pigeon, another classic of his, in a 
sweet-tinged combination.
30:19:
The olive oil comes from Spain's centuries-old olive trees and carries 
the signature of the also double-starred — and very 
green — chef Ricard Camarena. Everything is sophisticated, nothing flashy. 
The setting, discreet, features a brick wall that still 
houses old bread ovens. Service is attentive, 
without excess (phew). A tranquil experience, 
accompanied by wines and finished on a sweet note of tangerine, 
almond, verbena — and honeycomb bites. ■

AFFINEMENT. The French word for refinement is 
exactly right for the iconic French restaurant on the 
left bank of the Seine. When you think of elegance,

Tour d'Argent — and the weight of this name.

The restaurant grew on that corner, starting small 
and buying up the buildings above and beside. The trajectory

an aura of aristocracy, just like the traditional 
service, the silver service plates, the crystal water carafe.
31:19:
Beyond privacy. There's no coming and going. It's not 
to see and be seen. The regulars recognize the value of 
what they will find and know what it's about: good food and 
good service. The ritual of eating without rushing and without looking at the 
phone. The 400-plus-page wine list is 
brought by the sommelier, three waiters assemble the plate

I ordered the four-course tasting menu. Of an unbelievable

Duck is not the kind of thing I'd normally order,

“THERE'S NO COMING AND GOING. IT'S NOT 
TO SEE AND BE SEEN. THE REGULARS 
RECOGNIZE THE VALUE OF WHAT 
THEY WILL FIND AND KNOW WHAT 
IT'S ABOUT: GOOD FOOD AND  
GOOD SERVICE”
32:8:
Crossing the world for a fish broth in 
Vietnam, an octopus in Galicia, a tiradito in 
Lima or a tucunaré in Belém. Heading to Bangkok 
for a green curry, crossing Valencia for a 
paella, catching a train in Piedmont for a 
tajarin with fresh truffles. In restaurants with 
a strong tie to local traditions, in others 
that practice the fusion of different cultures 
or in those that prioritize contemporary 
techniques. Traveling to eat is choosing 
the path of flavor — places where the dishes 
give the taste of the place. Destination dining 
experiences give travelers more reasons to 
go and to return.
32:9:
By Mauro Marcelo Alves  
with contribution by Juliana A. Saad
32:11: The HYPE | F O O D I E S
32:12:
Eating has always been a legitimate reason to leave home — or 
cross continents. From the choreographed banquets at Versailles 
to the nomadic markets of the Silk Road, the world also 
moves around the table. Romans sought oysters from 
Gaul, olive oils from Hispania, fermented sauces from Egypt. 
Frenchmen rode to the cassoulet of Castelnaudary. 
In feudal Japan, the feudal lords — or daimyos — traveled 
with their cooks in search of the right ingredients. 
In Brazil, muleteers opened routes with salt and flour; 
sailboats stitched flavors between islands, ports and villages.
32:13:
Then came the guides — and the criteria. Michelin, created 
in 1900 to help French motorists find 
good places to eat and sleep, became 
the most influential gastronomic rating 
system in the world, adopting expressions such as “méritent le 
voyage,” or “worth the journey,” for restaurants 
with the maximum three stars.
32:14:
The guide has been expanding its picks into markets 
previously overlooked, such as Colombia or secondary 
provinces of China. And it may be a clear showing that 
quality restaurants are not the exclusive privilege of big 
cities: Paz, by chef Poul Andrias Ziska, with two stars, 
sits in Tórshavn, capital of the isolated Faroe Islands, a 
city of 22,000 inhabitants.
32:15:
Lately, The World's 50 Best Restaurants has set the tone 
for new revelations. On the 2025 list, Brazil 
appears in the main ranking with Rio's Lasai (more 
on that on p.56). Here at home, Guia Quatro Rodas, from 
1965 to 2014, traveled the country researching restaurants 
in every region. I had the good fortune of editing the Guide for 
ten years, discovering great food even in the most un-
likely places. Below I share world destinations where 
food is the very reason for journeys across the five continents.
33:3: The HYPE | F O O D I E S
33:5:
In 2025, gastronomic tourism generated 
nearly US$ 1 trillion and already accounts for up to 35% 
of spending on high-end travel. It is one of 
the fastest-growing segments in the luxury sector, 
with projected growth of 17.3% through 2034, 
according to the World Food Travel Association.
33:6:
It is tradition, but it is also a trend. Films like 
Julie & Julia, Tampopo, Eat, Pray, Love, The 
Trip or the countless food-focused documentaries 
that made chef Anthony Bourdain famous, 
for instance, have turned the gesture into narrative 
— kitchens as destination, dishes as itinerary, 
restaurants as leading locations.
33:7:
According to the World Food Travel Association, 
travelers are increasingly willing to
33:8:
design itineraries around menus, chefs and 
ingredients. Data shows that hotels with 
award-winning restaurants saw a 40% 
increase in international bookings in 2024. And 
the figures mirror the movement in our country, 
with gastronomic tourism growing 40% over the 
last five years.
33:9:
More than 60% of Brazilian travelers choose 
destinations on the strength of events like the 
Tiradentes Gastronomy Festival, Rio Gastronomia 
or the Bahia Festival Cultura e Sabores. And the 
luxury hospitality market is paying close attention, 
creating experiences ranging from fishing with 
local chefs to menus aboard private boats in 
Paraty or Trancoso. All to leave the journey with 
a lingering appetite for more.

In Micronesia and encompassing more than 340 islands, Palau is a pioneer 
in regenerative tourism, and Blue Corner is its most famous dive 
site. The reef wall drops onto an underwater plateau where everything happens at once: sharks, 
turtles, rays and giant tuna circulate among schools of fish.
42:10:
▸ BEST TIME: November to May (excellent visibility 
and calmer seas)
42:11:
▸ PRESERVATION: Palau established the Pacific's first Marine 
World Heritage zone, banned industrial fishing 
across much of its territory and requires every visitor 
to sign an “ecological pledge” upon entering the country.
42:12:

fect for diving, or snorkeling, at Blue 
Corner. Submerged caves and blue holes are also 
within reach — the yacht has a smaller boat to take you to 
any dive site. A marine biologist on board 
teaches about conservation.

Cod Hole is known for the giant potato cod that

zones, bans anchoring in sensitive 
areas and continually invests in reef regeneration.

vation projects.

Aurora Expeditions 
Founded in 1991 by environmentalists Greg and Margaret Mortimer, 
Aurora Expeditions takes concrete steps to help create 
a positive impact on the planet. It has officially become a B Cor-
porationTM — a certification that reflects transparency and sustainabi-
lity in business practices — and has operated since 2021 as a 
carbon-neutral business. Its three innovative ships (Greg Mor-
timer, Sylvia Earle and Douglas Mawson) feature advanced 
technologies; their virtual anchoring system allows the ships 
to hold position in the water without dropping a physical anchor, 
protecting delicate seabeds. During the voyages (iti-
neraries can lead to destinations as varied as Antarctica, Sval-
bard, Indonesia and Borneo, or the Mediterranean!), specialists in 
areas such as marine biology and environmental science share 
their knowledge with travelers.

47:4: By Bianca Piragibe and Bruno Spiguel
47:5:
Humankind's capacity to create beauty is something that 
never ceases to impress. Temples carved into rock, sacred and secular 
arts, cities that are like jewels, canals, palaces, 
pyramids! The magnificent creations that reveal the best of 
human creativity have been, ever since tourism exists, one of the most 
compelling reasons to travel. Without a doubt, they will continue to be. For 
some of these works, however, the clock ticks differently.
47:6:
These are places that, at risk of flooding, overtourism, desertification, 
and a list of other perils and threats, may be lost — or 
disfigured — in the coming decades or centuries. More than 
ever, we must draw attention to them and to this cause: 
take a stand, call for resources, investments and effective actions, 
so that they can survive. And, in all their glory, fascinate 
future generations, just as they fascinate us.
47:7:
From prehistoric art to the Sydney Opera House, passing through Persepolis, 
Venice and Petra, follow this map of monuments 
created by humanity that today are under threat. If we 
are capable of creating so much beauty, we must be capable 
of protecting it.
47:14:
Governmental and civil institutions, 
universities, researchers, residents: many are, 
and need to be, involved in fighting 
this righteous battle. Two deserve the spotlight: 
UNESCO and the World Monuments Fund 
(WMF), which promote research, restorations 
and visibility, helping to attract new 
investors committed to solutions. 
Among them, the Accor group, which is helping  
in the conservation of at-risk sites,  
such as the historic water systems of Bhuj,  
in India, where the use of interconnected  
canals, reservoirs and wells 
that capture rainwater will 
be restored to combat 
the severe water crisis  
facing the region; and the 
Sorbonne Chapel, on p. 100.

48:3:
The Treasury is a fascination carved into rock. The building was constructed during the reign of 
Aretas IV (9 BC to 40 AD), leader of the Nabataeans, and served funerary purposes. Its 39.5-meter facade, 
sculpted in sandstone under Hellenistic influences, features a figure of the goddess 
Isis surrounded by Amazon warriors. According to a Bedouin legend, it was there that a pharaoh is said to have 
hidden his treasures. Now you know the origin of the name by which the place is known.
48:4:
UNDER THREAT: Climate change has increased floods and rainfall, which has 
amplified erosion and created structural risk to the buildings across the entire complex.
48:5:
IN ACTION: Studies and projects are underway at the site, including plans to rebuild the 
ancient Nabataean canal system to prevent flooding.
48:7:
It is almost as Jorge Ben Jor sings: a monument 
in tribute to love, a gift from prince Shah Jahan to his beloved Ilyiu Mahal. Or, in 
correct spelling, Mumtaz Mahal. In her memory was 
erected this stunning mausoleum, with striking use 
of white marble and precious stones, the jewel of Indo-Islamic 
architecture.
48:8:
UNDER THREAT: The marble from Rajasthan has an en-
chanting effect. It appears pink in the early morning and gradually tur-
ns whiter throughout the day. But this facade 
deteriorates, darkening and losing its shine, under 
the effects of pollution. And this has worsened with 
the rise in droughts, caused by heat waves 
reaching 50º C. Its structure, including the founda-
tion, also suffers from poor maintenance.
48:9:
IN ACTION: The exterior of the building undergoes 
recurring cleaning. But more structural interventions 
have been implemented at a slow pace, such as the 
ban on polluting industries and vehicles in the area.
48:11:
The Necropolis of Memphis houses 38 pyramids. Among them, the
famed three at Giza. The Great Pyramid of Khufu is the sole sur-
viving wonder of the ancient world's seven, still standing since
2560 BC, and one of humanity's most significant monuments.
The pyramids served as tombs for royalty, preserving bodies and
goods for use in the afterlife, as Egyptian beliefs of the era
taught.
48:12:
UNDER THREAT: Did you know they originally shone under the
sun, reflected off the polished white limestone that clad them?
They may also have had granite bases, but the materials were
plundered over time, along with much of their treasure. Looting
is in the past, but today's list is long: groundwater seepage
from leaking irrigation canals, massive urbanization of the area,
overtourism, and even restorations that don't follow global
guidelines.
48:13:
IN ACTION: Private vehicle traffic has been banned around the
main pyramids, and archaeologists and Egyptologists managed to
prevent the disfigurement of the Pyramid of Menkaure, which the
government intended to clad in granite (a move that would have violated its authenticity).
49:2: Planet | C O N S E R V A Ç Ã O
49:4:
Terracotta
sculptures at
Alcobaca
Monastery  
Portugal
49:5:
Between the 17th and 18th
centuries, Cistercian monks
at Alcobaca used the abun-
dant clay found in the region
to create a sacred master-
piece: the monastery holds a
collection of 179 terracotta
sculptures in the Baroque
style.
49:6:
UNDER THREAT: Susceptible
to temperature shifts, the
fragile statues can crack and
warp.
49:7:
IN ACTION: Plans are in place
to train local artisans in the
craft of restoration.
49:9:
It holds "outstanding universal value." It is a "uni-
que archaeological site." UNESCO's words! Begun
in 518 BC, Persepolis was the capital of the Per-
sian Empire and carries a Mesopotamian style.
The palatial complex covers 13 hectares with ma-
jestic staircases, elegant columns, throne rooms,
sculpted friezes, immense winged bulls...
49:10:
UNDER THREAT: Erosion, intensified by pollu-
tion and the rise in extreme rainfall, is erasing
the precious high reliefs. In addition, the rapid
subsidence of Iran's soil, a consequence of the
irrigation system depleting aquifers, puts much
of the country at risk, including Persepolis.
49:11:
IN ACTION: The debate has only recently become
public, and there is no record of solutions being
set in motion, just incipient studies on better
water use in the arid region.
49:16:
One of the most celebrated architectural landmarks of the 20th century
materializes the revolutionary ideas of Danish architect Jorn Utzon.
The roof of organic lines, inspired by sailboats, is formed by "shells"
clad with more than a million tiles imported from Sweden.
49:17:
UNDER THREAT: Rising temperatures are expected to raise the
sea level along the waterfront which, combined with the increased
frequency of storms that cause sudden tidal surges, puts its
structure at risk. The risk of extreme heat is also a concern.
49:18:
IN ACTION: Climate events are being monitored to inform future
adaptation measures, such as building cooling strategies.
50:1: Planet | C O N S E R V A Ç Ã O
50:11:
A remarkable series of temples
carved into rock, with prayer
halls hewn by hand, where
Buddhist spirituality and art
were transmitted in ancient
China. The Maijishan grottoes,
dating from the 4th and 5th
centuries, preserve more than
7,000 sculptures and murals.
50:12:
UNDER THREAT: The grottoes
are suffering deterioration
caused by mass tourism. One
million visitors a year, reaching
13,000 a day in high season.
And they are not alone — there
are more rock-cut temples in
the country under the same
threat.
50:13:
IN ACTION: Restoration projects
and studies on sustainable tourism
management are underway.
50:15:
Lining up 15 moai in a row, Tongariki is the most im-
posing ahu (ancient ceremonial platform) on Easter
Island. The intriguing statues of compacted volcanic
ash, carved by the Rapa Nui people likely between
1250 and 1500 AD, stand between 5.6 and 8.7 meters
tall. They are believed to represent deeply cherished
ancestors.
50:16:
UNDER THREAT: With climate change, erosion has in-
creased on all moai. This ahu, very close to the coast,
may become one of the first victims of rising waters.
A date has even been forecast: the year 2080.
50:17:
IN ACTION: The island, with limited resources, relies
on the support of international organizations that have
applied various technologies and chemical treatments
to help combat erosion. A containment wall has been
built on one or two other platforms.
50:19:
The city, which was born as a temporary refuge for peasants from
barbarian invasions, became a triumph of engineering. What sus-
tains the medieval campaniles and palazzos for about 1,600 years
are countless wooden pilings driven into the muddy floor of the
lagoon. Submerged, the stakes do not rot because, isolated from
oxygen and saturated with minerals, they have "petrified" over
time.
50:20:
UNDER THREAT: Overtourism and projected sea level rise
haunt Venice.
50:21:
IN ACTION: Bans on large ships at its port and visitor fees
are among the tourism regulation measures. For protection
against high tides, the Mose system already exists — movable
barriers that isolate the lagoon from the sea. Yet, designed
to be used five times a year, it has been activated around 100
times since 2020, due to climate change. As a result, it too
becomes a threat, as the frequent isolation of the lagoon alters
its ecosystem and pollutes it.
50:23:
It was still prehistory when the
world's most sophisticated meg-
alithic monument — made of
stones — began to rise. Menhir
by menhir, or in other words,
stone by stone, this space was
created for ceremonial uses and
astronomical studies, about
which much is imagined, but
very little is known.
50:24:
UNDER THREAT: The increase
in the number and intensity of
floods endangers the ancestral
structure. Infrastructure projects
nearby are another risk, as is
vandalism.
50:25:
IN ACTION: What mattered in
this case was inaction. Experts
succeeded in halting the con-
struction of a tunnel in the area,
which could have destabilized
the stones.
51:2: Planet | C O N S E R V A Ç Ã O
51:5:
Part of Sorbonne Universite, the 17th-century Chapel of Saint Ursula
is one of the most iconic buildings in the Quartier Latin. The mural
painted on the dome and the sculpted tomb are its greatest treasures.
51:6:
UNDER THREAT: Structural damage and conservation problems kept
the chapel closed for decades.
51:7:
IN ACTION: A renovation project, covering both the structure and the
artworks, is underway. The plan is for it to reopen.
51:9:
The breathtaking moun-
tain pass, the 15th-century
ruins, the stone terraces,
the history, the architec-
ture integrated with astro-
nomical knowledge: it is
the sum of many factors
that makes it one of the
best-known archaeolog-
ical sites on the planet.
Built by the Inca empire
and abandoned after the
arrival of the Spanish in
the 16th century, it was
"rediscovered" only in 1911
and, since then, has been
on many a bucket list.
51:10:
UNDER THREAT: The new
international airport be-
ing built in Chinchero wor-
ries archaeologists and
residents alike, as it will
allow a much greater
influx of tourists. The pos-
sibility of unchecked
growth of new tourist
structures in the area
adds concern.
51:11:
IN ACTION: The local gov-
ernment has set the daily
visitor limit at 4,500 for the
citadel, rising to 5,600 in
high season and selected
holidays. There are also
limits for those arriving
via the trails.
51:13:
Lady Liberty Enlightening the World, the American
postcard icon, was a gift from France to the United
States. Installed in 1886, for decades she was one of
the first sights for immigrants arriving in the country
through the port of New York: a total of 12 million
people. You can climb up to her crown — 162 spiral
steps. Clad in copper sheets (now oxidized and, for
that reason, green), her internal structure was signed
by Alexandre-Gustave Eiffel. Yes, the Tower guy.
51:14:
UNDER THREAT: Her location makes her an easy vic-
tim of the growing number of storms, floods, and
hurricanes.
51:15:
IN ACTION: There is debate around solutions for ris-
ing tide levels, but this is not a pressing danger, given
the height (47 meters) of her pedestal.
51:21:
ONE-THIRD OF WORLD HERITAGE
CITIES LIE ALONG THE COAST, AT
RISK FROM RISING OCEANS
51:22:

52:10:
Mandarin Oriental, Paris
GOOD FOR THE TRAVELER: In a US$ 3.5 billion project, the iconic
hotel stretches across one hundred hectares along the Arabian Sea.
Its dome, reaching 200 meters high, complements the gold-leafed
ceilings and the more than a thousand Swarovski crystal chandeliers —
the largest weighing 2.5 tons. From the flawless 24-hour butler service
to the catamarans on hand, plus restaurants like the Michelin-starred
Italian Talea, sophistication is reflected in every detail.
52:11:
GOOD FOR ALL: The hotel offers accommodations free of animal-
derived products, plant-based dining menus, and vegan amenities by
VOTARY. The commitment to animal welfare extends to the spa, with
certified vegan treatments, and to the beach club: the towels are
bamboo-derived, and the sunscreen is 100% plant-based.
52:12:
GOOD FOR THE TRAVELER: Inspired by 1930s Art Deco, this historic address reveals interiors that breathe
Parisian haute couture and savoir-faire (note the 135 Swarovski butterflies decorating the hotel's entrance, a re-
ference to its number of rooms). Celebrating the French terroir, the restaurant Camelia, by Maxime Gilbert, offers
menus with local, seasonal ingredients in a setting full of natural light and views of the beautiful 450-sqm indoor garden.
In summer, tables mingle with the garden's flowers and trees for al fresco meals in the spirit of Parisian bistros.
52:13:
GOOD FOR ALL: To boost the bee population in Paris, four hives were installed on the hotel's rooftop more
than a decade ago. The project, in partnership with beekeeper Audric de Campeau, a reference in the city,
has created a pesticide-free space marked by the hum of 1,200 bees that pollinate, fertilize, energize,
and revitalize the surrounding nature.

54:4: Horizons | T R A V E L  J O U R N A L  |  C a m b o d i a
54:6:
FEW COUNTRIES EMBODY WITH SUCH BEAUTY 
THE SYMBOLISM OF THE LOTUS FLOWER AS 
CAMBODIA. BORN FROM THE MUD, THE SACRED FLOWER 
RISES ABOVE THE SURFACE — PURE, LUMINOUS, 
SERENE. AND SO IT IS WITH THE COUNTRY: AFTER DECADES 
MARKED BY PAIN, BY THE SHADOW OF THE 
KHMER ROUGE REGIME, BY THE FORCED SILENCES 
OF CIVIL WAR, CAMBODIA ROSE AGAIN. AND NOT 
MERELY SURVIVED: IT FLOURISHED.
54:7:
What I found there was an incredibly kind people, with open 
smiles, who welcome with generosity even while carrying in 
memory a recent history of suffering. There is a spirituality 
that runs through everything — from the ancient temples to the simplest 
daily life, from the saffron-robed monks to the aroma of jasmine 
rice. To visit Cambodia is to witness the strength of overcoming. 
It is to see up close how life has the capacity to reinvent itself. It is 
to feel that, like the lotus, beauty blooms even when everything 
around seems to have no meaning.
55:5:
THE FIRST BREATH
We arrive in Cambodia like someone stepping softly so 
as not to wake the gods asleep within the temples.
55:6:
A simple gesture that transforms arrival into welcome:
I strongly recommend booking the Meet & Greet. The
visa, baggage claim, the walk through immigration, all
is done with fluidity and efficiency, and inside a modern,
spacious, and surprisingly elegant airport —
Siem Reap International Airport opened in October 2023.
With this experience, there is no waiting:
in minutes you are already face to face with your driver.
My transfer was handled by Amansara, of the iconic Aman
group. A driver with a continuous smile awaited me
with precise information and Wi-Fi connection. Every detail
designed to dissolve any remnant of travel fatigue.
55:7:
THROUGH THE WINDOW
The first glimpse of the country comes through the car 
window, as one might watch a sacred book being 
opened. The drive to the hotel takes about 50 minutes. 
A span of time that, far from being an inconvenience, serves 
to carry you from the outside world into the singular 
atmosphere of Cambodia. A perfect blend of hospitality 
and contemplation that already sets the tone of the journey. 
Nothing feels rushed, even amid the apparent chaos. There is 
traffic, yes. Motorbikes in swarms, entire families on 
two wheels, cars swerving with millimetric calm, 
tuk-tuks weaving across the asphalt, yet everything moves in a 
nearly choreographed rhythm. The scenery mixes the urban with 
a still-rural air: simple houses, improvised markets, 
colorful little flags. It is not a place that tries 
to impress — it is a place that simply is. And perhaps 
for that very reason, it impresses so deeply.
55:8:
ARRIVING AT AMANSARA
The gates open and the world outside disappears. A former
royal residence built to host King Sihanouk's guests,
the hotel preserves that aura of contained elegance
— noble, but never ostentatious. It is a luxury that does not raise
its voice. The modernist lines of the original architecture have been
respected and softened by an Aman aesthetic: pure lines,
neutral palette, presence of nature at every angle.
55:9:
Reception happens without a counter, without stiff
protocol. A tea and the smile of someone who understands
that something different begins there. In every corner, a delicate surprise,
like the collection of Buddhas scattered with a refined eye
and aesthetic sensibility. They are unique pieces, of different
sizes and expressions. You feel like taking them all
home — and if you want, they take care of that too: there is
door-to-door shipping service.
55:10:
The iconic central pool is the heart of the hotel. Around
it, paths lead to elegant suites, small internal
courtyards, and a spa with an almost monastic atmosphere.
Staying at Amansara is reconnecting with what is
essential and beautiful.
55:11:
HOW TO BEGIN:  
IN SILENCE, AT THE SPA
For those arriving from afar, crossing time zones and realities,
there is no better way to begin. A massage
upon arrival is a way of telling the body: now
you are here. And here, everything has another rhythm. In a space
of natural light, the treatments follow ancestral
principles, blending Eastern medicine with Aman
refinement. Nothing is excessive — not the pressure of the hands, not
the aroma of herbs and oils, not the music. The body is cared
for as if it were an extension of the soul.
55:12: Horizons | T R A V E L  J O U R N A L  |  C a m b o d i a
56:5:
IN SIEM REAP: THE SOUND, THE SCENT, THE STREET
On the first night, Amansara's silence gives way to the
vibrant buzz of Siem Reap. And the best way to live out
this contrast is to hop on the private tuk-tuk the hotel
provides, with a dedicated driver. He waits at the door,
with soft lanterns, scented towels, and chilled water.
56:6:
The streets buzz: the central market mixes 
popular stalls with improbable antique shops, bars in a row, 
colored lights, music from all sides. Rummaging 
there is a ritual — there are incredible pieces if you have a keen 
eye, among stone statues, embroidered fabrics and objects 
with history. All wrapped in the unmistakable aroma of 
Cambodia: jasmine, smoke, pepper and something sweet 
in the air that one cannot quite place.
56:7:
On the way back, dinner at Chanrey Tree restaurant; charming,
by the river, with a welcoming setting and traditional
Cambodian cuisine prepared with care. The flavors are
intense, authentic, and the service gentle, like everything in this
country. A tip: watch out for the chili — they take it
seriously. It is a night to feel the pulse of the place. To see
people. To get a little lost. And to return to Amansara with
your soul still humming and your body ready to sleep in
the most absolute tranquility.
56:8: Horizons | T R A V E L  J O U R N A L  |  C a m b o d i a
56:9:
Since I work in tourism, it's part of my job to
know different styles of accommodation. After
two (amazing!) days at Amansara, which arranged
the tours on the next page for me,
I stayed for two more nights at the Park Hyatt
Siem Reap. A completely different proposition,
but equally special. The hotel is more
contemporary, good for families and groups of
friends, in the heart of the city. Excellent service,
great rooms, and a perfect location to
discover Siem Reap on foot. At night, the central
courtyard becomes a setting for cultural performances:
traditional dances, live music. Try to
reserve a table for dinner in the courtyard and enjoy
the show at leisure. The spa also deserves
a mention, with treatments worth the pause.
It is another rhythm, more connected to the movement of
the city — and it was great to live both sides.
56:10:
PARK HYATT  
SIEM REAP:  
ANOTHER VIEW,  
ANOTHER RHYTHM
57:3: Horizons | T R A V E L  J O U R N A L  |  C a m b o d i a
58:1:
THE DAY THE  
GODS WHISPER
Waking early at Amansara has a purpose: to be
among the first to pass through the gates of the lost
empire and visit the temples of Angkor. By 7:30 a.m.,
the crowds have not arrived. That is when the day begins,
and not just any day, but one of those that seem
written in another ink, in another language, in a
parallel dimension between stone, jungle, and spirit.
58:2:
My guide, BunThorn, was waiting for me with the calm 
only someone who grew up among temples can have. 
Kind-hearted and deeply knowledgeable, he was the one 
who introduced me to sacred Cambodia — not as a 
history lesson, but as a meditative walk through places 
where the past still breathes.
58:3:
We started at Ta Prohm, perhaps the most cinematic
of the temples, famous for its appearance
in Tomb Raider. It has been surrendered to time, and giant
trees merge with the walls, as if
wanting to protect the ruins or claim them
back. The roots flow like organic sculptures,
embracing doorways, crossing the ceiling. We
entered from the east side, where there was hardly
anyone. It was like discovering a secret.
58:4:
From there, we went on to Angkor Thom, the ancient
royal capital. The Terrace of the Elephants, with its
stone reliefs, looks like a procession frozen
in time. The grounds of the Royal Palace still exude a
certain solemnity. But it is at Bayon Temple that
the soul expands. Fifty-four towers, each carved
with four serene faces of Buddha, facing
the cardinal points. They are said to have been inspired
by King Jayavarman VII himself, the emperor
who built the temple. A symbolic portrait of his
ideal of compassion. They watch you with an
acceptance so deep that you want to stay
there for hours, simply being.
58:5:
In the carvings of the lower galleries, scenes of daily
life from the 12th century: markets, battles, fishermen,
festivities, animals. Everything is so meticulous, so
human. It moves you. Among Buddhist figures, curiously,
Hindu gods appear — traces of a
time when the two religions did not diverge,
but intertwined.
58:6:
On the way back, a visit to a small monastery, where
I took part in a Buddhist blessing ceremony.
Monks chanted while tying
a white cotton bracelet around my wrist,
wishing me health, peace, and a good journey. It was
brief, but unforgettable. I returned to the hotel with my eyes
full and my heart even fuller.
58:7:
FROM THE GOLD OF  
MORNING TO TWILIGHT
The next day also begins early. Very early.
At 5 a.m. — but what wakes you is the promise of the sub-
lime. Still wrapped in twilight, we head
to the Angkor Archaeological Park. Arriving early
means witnessing one of the most symbolic
spectacles of Southeast Asia: the sunrise behind
the towers of Angkor Wat, reflected in the mirror
ponds behind the temple.
58:8:
Few experiences have this combination of
scale, light, and meaning. The sky shifts from deep
blue to gold. It is as if the temple wakes
with the day — and, suddenly, everything seems much
larger than you.
58:9:
And it really is. Angkor Wat is the largest religious
monument in the world, built in the 12th century. On the
walls, carvings depict scenes from Hindu mythology:
apsara dancers, celestial realms. The walk
is long, but you don't feel
tired. What you feel is
awe. Admiration. Gratitude for
the existence of something like this.
58:10:
Horizons | T R A V E L  D I A R Y  |  C A M B O D I A
Art as devotion
58:18:
READ MORE 
ABOUT 
ANGKOR WAT 
ON P. 206
59:3:
THE CAMBODIA THAT TRANSFORMS: 
ART, CRAFT AND PURPOSE
Between one temple and another, there is another Cambodia pulsing — the 
one of hands that create, of projects that care, of initiatives 
that show that tourism can (and should) have a positive 
impact. The first I came to know was Refill Not Landfill, a 
simple yet powerful initiative to reduce plastic consumption with 
(beautiful!) reusable bottles, distributed free 
at hotels (such as Aman) and partner locations, 
with refill stations across the country.
59:4:
I also visited Artisans Angkor, a benchmark in
Siem Reap. They train young Cambodians in traditional crafts
like stone carving, silk, painting, and sculpture — crafts
that nearly disappeared during the
Khmer Rouge regime. Beyond the atelier, there are several
shops in the city. To take home something authentic and with direct
impact, it is an excellent choice.
59:5:
And at night, one of the projects that most moved me: the
Phare, The Cambodian Circus, which was born from a
school founded by artists who worked with orphans
and young war survivors. Many of those who step onto the
stage found a new life there. It is not about
pyrotechnics — it is a show about truth. Don't expect
Cirque du Soleil, but rather hope in the form of art.
59:6:
Getting to know this side of the country transforms everything. It is what links
the sacred to the everyday, the past to the future — and the traveler
to what truly matters.
59:7:
NO JUDGMENT
I traveled to Cambodia with my husband. It may sound like a simple 
sentence — and it is. But, in some places in the world, it still calls 
for some care. As part of the LGBTQIAPN+ community, we have 
learned to observe. To sense, even before any word is spoken, how 
we will be seen, treated, read. And there we felt no judgment at all. 
No tension. Only presence. And respect. Perhaps because Buddhism, 
which is everywhere, does not judge. It also observes. It teaches 
silence and the inner path. But it neither imposes truths nor condemns 
affection. In the blessings we received, in the visits to the temples, in 
the glances we crossed in the streets, there was no censure, no 
discomfort, not even curiosity. There was naturalness.
59:8:
FAREWELL
Working in travel, I've learned to handle arrivals and depar-
tures well. It's routine: land, live the place — and move on. Almost automatic. 
But this time, it wasn't. Cambodia moved me in a way that's hard 
to explain. Perhaps it was the blend of grandeur and deli-
cacy, the silence of the temples and the smile of the people, what reveals itself and 
what is simply felt. Perhaps the spirituality that lingers in the air. Or 
the contrast between a past so harsh and a present so full of kind-
ness and joy. All I know is that, as I hadn't felt in a long time, 
it was hard to leave this place. And perhaps that is the greatest sign that the 
journey was special: when, upon departing, a part of you remains.  [LT]
59:9: Horizons | T R A V E L  J O U R N A L  |  C a m b o d i a
59:10:
For those curious to know another
side of the country — less filtered — the Vespa tour
through the countryside is a great experience. The structure
of the tour is excellent, with modern Vespas. But it is
worth saying: this is not a luxury tour. It is about
authenticity. Not for everyone.
59:11:
We head toward the countryside passing through simple villages,
plantations. The tour begins at APOPO,
where we meet the famous "hero rats" trained
to locate landmines. My first question was whether they die in the process. The
answer? No. They are too light to set off the
explosives. And they are very well cared for. A
highly touristy program, but worthwhile for those interested
in topics of social impact.
59:12:
We stop at temples, receive Buddhist blessings,
and visit families who keep traditional crafts alive.
We learn about the artisanal production of palm
sugar, made from the sap of palm trees, and sticky rice,
steamed and served on banana leaves.
All simple, but made with dedication and affection.
59:13:
At the local market, shapes and smells, not all pleasant
to our Western eyes and sense of smell. But this is
life as it is. Real food, in a country that
does not try to hide its essence. Between stops,
a smiling artisan was turning straw into delicate,
useful pieces. Along the way, children leaving school
and waving at us. Real. True. Simple.
Not for everyone. And very special.
59:14:
NOT FOR EVERYONE: BY  
VESPA THROUGH ROYAL CAMBODIA

60:7:
ICEBERGS SCULPTED INTO CONTEMPORARY ART,
ORCAS SLICING THROUGH THE WATERS,
GLACIERS COLLAPSING THUNDEROUSLY,
POLAR BEARS BLENDING INTO THE WHITE OF THE
HORIZON, AND CELESTIAL LIGHTS STEALING THE SHOW.
60:8:
Dramatic by nature, the world's largest island slowly reveals
a stunning landscape, among fjords and ice floes,
all the way to remote villages that seem frozen
in other eras. Greenland lies along the polar line of
the North American continent between the North Atlantic and
Arctic oceans. Venturing through its white vastness is a
guarantee of witnessing an extreme spectacle. Its inhospitable territory —
belonging to the Kingdom of Denmark — hides treasures beneath
layers of ice carved by fabulous fjord systems.
The result you can already imagine. Cinematic.
61:1: Horizons | G R E E N L A N D
61:5:
Immense icebergs welcomed me on Greenland's southeast coast. In an arm of sea
of Skjoldungen Fjord squeezed between rugged walls, they drifted at
the wind's whim. Fjords are flooded valleys that come from ice ages to make us
catch our breath. They form with the retreat of glaciers, between high rocky mountains, followed
by flooding with seawater. The force of that landscape is so great that it makes anyone
feel like a drop in the ocean. A remote region, inhabited only from 1938 to 1965
by a group of Indigenous people from the frozen lands of Ammassalik. No more. And it was
exactly in this tucked-away corner of the planet that I first set foot in Greenland,
disembarking from a Swan Hellenic ship. A 5 km trek across rocks
and the fjord's tundra, the low-growing vegetation that dominates the region's biome, crowned
the day in tones of green, rare flowers, and a few little red berries. As soundtrack, the
birdsong. As for the cold, it wasn't even much: -10°C. After all, it was summer. And yes, the effects
of global warming are a reality. I felt it on my skin.
61:7:
Greenland is clearly a land of contrasts. It is enough to say it holds the title of
"world's largest island," at 2,170,000 sq km, yet it is covered by only 150 km of
roads and has a very low population density, with just 57,000 inhabitants. In total
there are six municipalities, embracing 17 cities and 57 villages. A giant territory,
comparable to the size of the states of Amazonas and Para combined, but practically
uninhabited. It is that two-thirds of Greenland lie above the Arctic Circle, with
anything but gentle temperatures that easily go past -40°C.
61:8:
That's why the inhabitants live mainly along the southwestern fjords, where the
climate is a little milder. Aappilattoq was where the magic of first encounter happened.
Unraveling this destination through the cultural traditions of its people is fascinating. With
a friendly population of 79, Aappilattoq was born in 1922 and loves receiving
visitors. A seemingly ordinary village — with colored little houses, a church, school, small
market, soccer field — were it not for its way of life based on ancestral customs and
its complete isolation. It is 20 minutes by helicopter from the nearest village.
61:9:
In this corner of the planet, they live basically from fishing and subsistence hunting.
Life is hard. They eat what the frozen nature of the Arctic offers, from birds to seals, and
use animal skins as part of the traditional dress, kalaallisuut. They even hunt
polar bear. An animal with such special status that it is represented in Greenland's
mythology and stamped on the national coat of arms. It is the most prestigious prey a
hunter can take down to feed his community. However, climate issues,
globalization, and modernization mean that the hunting that sustains the old traditions
is taking on new regulations.
62:1: Horizons | G R O E N L Â N D I A
63:1: Horizons | G R E E N L A N D
63:6:
Ice older than human history. It's easy to see that the best way to
explore its polar mantle is aboard an expedition ship. Sailing through
these sceneries is the certainty of revisiting an ice age where silence is broken only
by the sounds of nature and the resilience of the Inuit people. After a full day
of navigation, through a rough sea, the captain announced our arrival at Ivittuut,
at Cape Desolation. A true desolation to see the abandoned village. It was founded
in 1854, for the exploitation of a cryolite mine, a rare and valuable mineral extracted
until 1963 for the production of aluminum. It once had 700 inhabitants. Today it is a treasure
lost in southwest Greenland, where only the lowing of musk oxen
echoes among the houses.
63:7:
Not far from there, considering the island's vastness, lies the capital Nuuk. It
stretches gracefully along the coast of Greenland's second-largest fjord,
Nuup Kangerlua. It is beautiful, colorful, and tiny. It has a population of fewer than 20,000
inhabitants, mostly Danish. It is home to Greenland's only shopping center.
The city also has a university, airport, a lovely cultural center — named
Katuaq — in a building designed by Schmidt Hammer Lassen with undulations
mimicking the Nordic lights, and a museum worth visiting. The Qilakitsoq mummies stand out.
They were found by two hunters in 1972, but date back to 1475 and
are considered one of the most important archaeological finds in the Arctic.
63:9:
No expedition is complete without a stop in Ilulissat, the third-largest city,
with just over 4,000 inhabitants. Besides its charm, its many
museums (Icefjord Centre, Art Museum, and History Museum), air connections, and a few
hotels, it has the most impressive scenery in Greenland, the Ilulissat Icefjord, listed
as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It is where the Sermeq Kujalleq Glacier — one of
the largest in the Northern Hemisphere — pours gigantic blocks of ice into the fjord in a
staggering spectacle. Once detached from the glacier, the icebergs float monu-
mentally along a long corridor, until they meet the ocean. Some reach 300
meters high, with a small visible part above water and a world of ice
submerged. Icebergs the size of cathedrals. And it was exactly among those icebergs
that a group of humpback whales decided to show off while the sun bid its farewell. And as
if the surprises couldn't be more enchanting, the aurora borealis arrived stealth-
ily at nightfall, serpentining in greenish waves, in a celestial
phenomenon that takes the breath away.
64:1: Horizons | G R E E N L A N D
64:4:
An ancient tradition that remains a vital means of transportation for these people 
who live in frozen lands is dog sledding. These animals work hard and hold a 
significant place in local culture. Strict regulations exist to preserve the purity of the 
breed. In Sisimiut, no other dogs are permitted, ensuring there is no crossbreeding.
64:5:
Surrounded by dramatic mountains and breathtaking fjords, picturesque Sisimiut is the 
second-largest city in Greenland, with 5,600 inhabitants. Just below the Arctic Circle, 
the town pulses with wooden houses painted in vivid tones. During the colonial period, 
the color of each house carried an identifying message. Red for schools and churches, 
yellow for medical services, green for telecommunications, blue for fish factories and 
black for the police. This color code is fading into the past while the range of tones 
only grows. It's worth taking an ATV (all-terrain vehicle) to go further. The Arctic Circle 
Trail starts in Sisimiut and extends to Kangerlussuaq, an air base used during World 
War II and from where my return flight would depart. Through the plane's window I 
searched for details that could piece together the vast puzzle I had lived, for ten days, 
in this place where luxury is precisely the silence stirred by the force of isolation and 
by a beauty beyond the obvious. A remote vastness surrounded by untamed nature, 
immersive and captivating. A beauty as poetic as it is overwhelming. An experience 
worthy of a journey back in time, into the Ice Age. [LT]
64:6:
Gamla Höfnin, the old port of Reykjavík — capital 
of Iceland — was the starting point for the south 
coast of Greenland. I boarded the SH Vega, from 
the Swan Hellenic cruise line, focused on unconven-
tional destinations. Its 75 cabins accommodate 152 
passengers. On arrival, we were gifted two coats 
specially developed to withstand the frigid polar 
climate and waterproof boots with thermal protec-
tion (Muck Boots) to be used during the cruise.
64:7:
The interior was elegantly designed with clean lines 
and soft tones by a Scandinavian designer. The sui-
tes are spacious, well furnished with closets and 
have virtual-reality fireplaces. Some even have 
bathtubs. The ship also features a spa, a sauna with 
a panoramic window, two heated pools, a library, 
a well-equipped gym and two restaurants with deli-
cious cuisine. Essential comfort,
64:8:
since the first two days are al mare, without 
disembarking. Perfect for slowing down. Smooth 
sailing is guaranteed by cutting-edge equipment 
with powerful stabilizers designed for polar 
expeditions.
64:9:
Traveling to an extreme destination, more than a 
challenge, is about setting personal growth as a 
goal. The Observation Lounge gathers passengers 
throughout the ten days of the expedition. In this 
salon, outfitted with a bar, grand piano, small shop 
and large windows that bring the landscape to eye 
level, environmental care protocols and generous 
doses of knowledge from specialists are shared: 
biologists, naturalists, geologists and others. A 
highlight are the talks by Arielle Montgomery, a 
soul passionate about the country's culture and a 
strong voice spreading respect for the people of 
Greenland around the world.
65:6: Horizons | S C A N D I N A V I A
65:8:
uality of life. Plenty of green. A genuine commitment to sus-
tainability. The Nordic capitals have much in common — but 
each with its own personality. Oslo (Norway) has a calm pa-
ce of life and is a perfect synthesis of nature and urbanism. 
Copenhagen (Denmark), consistently voted one of the happiest cities in 
the world, combines minimalism with coziness and sustainability with plea-
sure. Cosmopolitan yet introspective, Stockholm (Sweden) is the embodiment 
of the Scandinavian lifestyle: beautiful, quietly luxurious and deeply connec-
ted to the environment. Another thread that binds them is the unmistakable, 
minimalist Scandinavian design. Furniture with simple, elegant and ergo-
nomic lines, light-wood objects and natural fabrics are everywhere: from 
hotels to cafés, from shops to museums. From lamps and armchairs to su-
permarket packaging, everything seems thought out with care and purpose. 
Among clean streets, striking gastronomy, open-air art and plenty of outdoor 
living, a journey through the Northern Capitals is an experience shaped by 
nature, design and lifestyle.
66:5: Horizons | S c a n d i n a v i a
66:6:
Between the serenity of the landscape and the boldness of 
contemporary architecture, Oslo, the capital of Norway, sur-
prises anyone expecting only a cold and reserved city. Com-
bining cutting-edge culture, Viking history, open-air art and 
one of the world's highest qualities of life, it has just over 
700,000 inhabitants, a compact center and efficient electric 
public transport that connects you to creative neighborhoods, 
parks, museums and the famous Oslo Fjord (which, despite 
the name, is what we'd call a bay). While walking, you notice 
the discreet elegance of Scandinavian design: from minima-
list cafés to the windows of concept stores.
66:7:
GASTRONOMY AND LOCAL LIFE
Oslo is undergoing a gastronomic revolution. Restaurants 
such as Maaemo (three Michelin stars plus a green star)
66:8:
take contemporary Nordic cuisine to another level. There 
are also great casual options, such as Vippa, a waterfront 
warehouse with international street food by the fjord, or 
Mathallen, a food market in Grünerløkka, Oslo's coolest 
neighborhood. A former industrial zone, it has become a 
stronghold of artists, cafés, thrift shops, vintage stores 
and alternative bars. For a coffee with Scandinavian 
charm, try Fuglen, which combines café, bar and vintage 
design shop.
66:9:
FORESTS AND TRAILS
Nature is always present: trails, forests, lakes and even 
ski stations 20 minutes from the center. The Nordmarka 
forest is the locals' backyard — perfect for hiking, 
mountain biking or cross-country skiing in winter.
66:15:
1.	 OPERA HOUSE (DEN NORSKE OPERA 
& BALLETT): With its iceberg-inspired 
architecture, it's a city icon. It's worth 
checking the program.
66:16:
2.	 MUNCH MUSEUM: Waterside, it holds 
the largest collection of painter Edvard 
Munch, including three versions of The Scream.
66:17:
3.	 VIGELAND PARK: The world's largest 
sculpture park made by a single artist, 
Gustav Vigeland. An open-air space 
mixing art, nature and reflection on the 
cycle of life. Unmissable.
66:18:
4.	 VIKING SHIP MUSEUM: Under 
renovation until 2027, it houses three 
authentic Viking ships and 9th-century objects.
66:19:

Tjuvholmen, a highlight is the Astrup Fearnley

contemporary art collection.
66:20:
6.	 AKERSHUS FORTRESS: A 13th-century

with museums and walks through 
corridors and ramparts.
66:21:
7.	 HOLMENKOLLEN: A bit outside

66:22:
8.	 FRAM MUSEUM AND KON-TIKI

66:23:
9.	 OSLO CITY HALL (RÅDHUS):  
Home of the Nobel Peace Prize, the

66:24:
10.	BOAT TOURS ON THE

67:4: Horizons | S c a n d i n a v i a
67:6:
Progressive and deeply connected to its roots, 
Copenhagen is a model of urbanism and quality 
of life. Residents value their time off and outdoor 
socializing. With just over 600,000 inhabitants, 
Copenhagen feels tailor-made for those seeking 
balance between culture and calm. As you walk its 
streets — or better, cycle them — you discover a city 
that lives at just the right pace: neither rushed nor 
still.
67:7:
HYGGE: THE DANISH  
SECRET TO HAPPINESS
One of the most widespread concepts in local 
culture is hygge — a word without a literal translation, 
yet one that captures the comfort, simplicity and 
well-being of everyday moments: being with friends 
at home, sharing wine by candlelight or warming up 
with a blanket in a cozy café. Hygge is everywhere, 
in the quiet neighborhoods, in the parks and even in 
the attitude of the locals.
67:8:
THE TASTE OF THE NEW  
NORDIC CUISINE
With a reimagined concept, Noma — five times 
number one on the 50 Best Restaurants list — helped 
reinvent cuisine. Copenhagen is today one of the 
world's most important gastronomic destinations: 
from the sophisticated, triple-starred Geranium to 
food halls like Reffen, with artisanal food stalls by 
the canal, and Torvehallerne, a modern market that 
brings together local delicacies like the famous 
smørrebrød (typical Danish open sandwich).
67:9:
A CITY THAT  
IS A LIFESTYLE
67:10:
1.	 NYHAVN: The city's most

commercial port, today it's a 
colorful canal lined with historic 
houses and moored boats.
67:11:
2.	 AMALIENBORG PALACE: 
Official residence of the royal 
family. The four identical 
palaces surround a square 
where the changing of the 
guard takes place.
67:12:
3.	 TIVOLI GARDENS: One

parks (since 1843), it combines

Don't miss the LEGO® store!
67:13:

where you can see the royal

67:14:
5.	 NATIONAL MUSEUM OF

palace, it's a deep dive into 
Danish history from the  
Viking era. 
6.	 THE LITTLE MERMAID (DEN

inspired by Hans Christian 
Andersen's tale is small,
67:15:
but emblematic. Worth  
a visit at dusk.
67:16:
7.	 ROUND TOWER

with a view of 
the center.
67:17:
8.	 CHRISTIANIA: An 
independent, alternative 
neighborhood known as the 
"Free City." A unique cultural

67:18:
9.	 DESIGNMUSEUM 
DANMARK: Denmark is 
synonymous with functional, 
elegant design, and this 
museum is the right place to

objects, fashion and

67:19:
10.	COPENHAGEN BY

Copenhagen is one of the most 
bike-friendly cities in

68:4: Horizons | S C A N D I N A V I A
68:5:
Known as the "Venice of the North," the city grew up among 
lakes and canals, and much of its charm comes from that 
liquid geography. Built on 14 islands connected by elegant 
bridges, the capital of Sweden blends Baroque palaces 
with minimalist architecture, tradition with avant-garde, 
nature with sophistication. What's more: everything works, 
everything is clean, everything seems made to last.
68:6:
FIKA, THERE WILL BE CAKE
Don't miss fika, the Swedish ritual of a coffee break with 
cakes and sweet buns (especially the kanelbulle, cinnamon 
bun). More than a snack, it's a philosophy of life. Great cafés 
for it: Vete-Katten, Fabrique and Drop Coffee. But there are 
also casual delights at the
68:7:
saluhall, covered markets like Östermalm's, ideal for 
tasting the iconic smörgåstårta (cake-shaped sandwich). 
For refined cuisine based on local, seasonal ingredients, 
Frantzén — among the world's best — is a memorable 
experience.
68:8:
URBAN WELL-BEING
Stockholm breathes nature. Half of its area is made up 
of parks, forests and canals. On sunny days, locals picnic 
or go boating. In summer, there are public spots to swim 
and sunbathe. In winter, ice-skating rinks and cozy 
cafés. [LT]
68:11:
1.	 GAMLA STAN: The medieval 
heart is one of Europe's best 
preserved. Its narrow alleys, 
colorful façades and cobblestone 
squares are home to 
cafés, craft shops and 
landmarks such as the Royal 
Palace, the monarchy's 
official residence; Stockholm 
Cathedral, the Gothic church 
where royal weddings and 
coronations are celebrated; and 
Stortorget, the photogenic 
central square.
68:12:
2.	 VASA MUSEUM: Displays a 
17th-century warship salvaged 
from the seabed after 333 years, 
with preserved details.
68:13:
3.	 ABBA THE MUSEUM: 
Interactive, fun and 
nostalgic. The museum 
dedicated to the world's most 
famous Swedish group features 
karaoke and original costumes.
68:14:
4.	 FOTOGRAFISKA: 
Photography museum with one of

the best cafés in the city.

the sea and the rooftops of 
Södermalm.
68:16:
5.	 MODERNA MUSEET: 
Houses works by Picasso, Dalí, 
Matisse, Duchamp and 
contemporary Swedish artists.
68:17:
6.	 DJURGÅRDEN: One of the 
greenest islands, perfect 
for relaxing. It's also where 
several attractions are located, 
including Skansen, an open-air

68:18:
7.	 SÖDERMALM: Creative, 
hip and bohemian neighborhood. 
Full of vegan cafés, 
design shops, thrift stores. 
Ideal for strolling and enjoying

a short trail with spectacular

68:20:
8.	 NORRMALM AND 
ÖSTERMALM: Neighborhoods 
for shopping, architecture and 
elegance. Norrmalm is home to

and department stores 
such as Åhléns. Östermalm,

68:21:
9.	 ART ON THE METRO: the 
metro network is called 
"the world's largest art 
gallery," with more than 90

Highlights: T-Centralen,

68:22:
10.	BOAT TOUR: 
Unmissable, whether through

archipelago with more than

Charming, friendly, pocket-sized. In Bergen, you do everything on foot. There's a cable-car ride. There's a restaurant I

the northern lights. But what really matters about the city isn't what it has, but what surrounds it — magnificent fjords and

where the scenic Flåmsbana train sets off on one of the world's most stunning routes, with countless waterfalls along the way.
69:9:
I recently watched the documentary Rebel Royalty: An Unlikely Love Story, about the love affair between the eldest daughter 
of the King of Norway and a Californian shaman. While I was cruising the fjords, I recognized the queen's yacht, which 
appears in the film. I mention this to speak of Norwegians' relationship with the fjords: they really do spend time there. 
Royalty or not. It's part of everyday life. For me, it was a dream. There are thousands of fjords, those long, narrow arms of 
sea, each more beautiful than the last. I saw them from above, from a cable car, and could already tell how special — 
almost untouched — this nature is. But to enter a fjord by boat, passing between the mountains, is what lets you feel that 
magnitude... that connection... that silence. Waterfalls, green plains, tiny houses, as if you were inside a painting.

In some of the wildest landscapes

TIERRA ATACAMA

IN A SILENT ACT OF LOYALTY, around 8,000 warriors 
have stood relentlessly still for two millennia, protecting 
the tomb of their leader: Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of 
unified China. Accidentally discovered in 1974, while 
farmers were trying to dig a well, the life-size statues 
that form the Terracotta Army are spread across 
archaeological pits within a complex of more 
than 55 km² on the outskirts of Xi'an. Each warrior displays its 
own individuality — whether through the sculpted beards or 
hairstyles, from armor-clad officers to kneeling 
archers. Terracotta horses and bronze chariots 
complete the scene. It's possible that Qin's "post-mortem empire" 
was shaped in the likeness of his dynasty's military organization. 
This UNESCO World Heritage site is one of the highlights of a 
journey to the Middle Kingdom; see our suggested itinerary on p. 144.

ideal for watching the sails against the blue sky and feeling the sea breeze all day long. If you prefer a

ON THE BANKS OF THE RHÔNE — which “flows in” through the window of several accommodations —, the Mandarin Oriental, Geneva draws 
inspiration from the river: its staircases feature frescoes narrating the river’s course, from its source in the Saint-Gothard massif to its mouth in 
the Camargue. The big news is the Ottolenghi restaurant, where Yotam Ottolenghi’s Mediterranean flavors (the chef behind 
London’s ROVI) take on Swiss twists in a seasonal, vegetable-forward menu. His root-to-tip approach offers dishes 
made on a live-fire grill paired with regional ingredients, such as Chällerhocker cheese and Les Miels de Stéphanie honey. At night, the 
terrace of the MO Bar is a must: beyond drinks based on herbal infusions and teas from India and East Asia, the lounge bar 
serves mocktails adapted for children, making it a great option for the whole family. The little ones will also 
love the Chocolate Paradise experience, which features a visit to Geneva’s most renowned chocolatiers.

faiths. Alongside fascinating museums — which help broaden

The planet's largest religious complex is 
Buddhist. In the 12th century, at the peak of the Khmer empire, 
King Suryavarman II ordered the construction 
of Angkor Wat in honor of the Hindu god 
Vishnu; for a time, Buddhism and Hinduism 
walked side by side. Its structural layout 
evokes a mandala. The main towers 
represent Mount Meru, center of the universe 
in Hindu and Buddhist cosmologies. It is also 
said there are alignments tied to the 
sun and astrology. On the walls, 
carvings depict epic battles, 
apsara dancers, celestial 
realms, ancient myths...

German baron Karl von Drais developed, in 1817, the 
first sketch of the bicycle: the draisine. It was wooden and 
hardly practical; the rider had to push off the ground with their feet 
to move forward. Pedals only appeared two decades later. 
In Victorian England, the penny-farthing model gained 
popularity — the one with an enormous front wheel 
and a much smaller rear one. It was from the 1880s on that 
bikes began to resemble today's. Then 
they took over the world, and it was a short leap to becoming 
yet another (delightful) way to travel. 
In 2024 alone, the cycling tourism 
market moved 
US$ 140 billion!

who sailed to places

Island and Madagascar. Beyond

ESSENTIALS, BY SOMEONE WHO HAS LOST

105:8:
M 
uch is said about how Amsterdam is vibrant, modern, 
progressive. There is talk of the red light district, of the 
singular relationship with drugs, of the nightlife. All of that is there, 
it is true. But, for someone coming from a city the size 
of São Paulo, spending a few days strolling among graceful 
canals, colorful 17th-century little houses, narrow tree-lined streets 
is like a stay in the countryside. In the best 
sense! Without the constant noise and traffic locking 
everything down, the quality of life is exemplary. For days cycling 
between museums, visiting fantastic restaurants, learning 
more of the history and eating stroopwafel — Amsterdam is 
a place to go, to be and to always return.
105:9:
BIKE
It's not just that the city is bike-friendly (and it super is). It's that 
cycling is woven into the culture and daily life of its inhabi-
tants. The bicycle is genuinely used as a means of 
transport, sometimes with attached carts for 
carrying small children (charming). The city, 
connected by more than 400 km of cycle paths, is their territory. 
To better feel Amsterdam's soul, nothing beats pedaling 
through it, through the parks, along the canals — and, along the 
way, watching its always casual, stylish residents.
105:10:
TULIPS
There are streaked ones, black ones. With many petals, ruffled. 
Opening into points, fringes, folds. Let yourself be 
surprised by the variety between mid-March and mid-
May, when they are everywhere in the city. It's worth visiting 
central Vondelpark, which creates floating arrangements of them in 
its ponds, the Flower Market, right across from De L’Europe, and the 
Tulip Museum, in a little canal house. Outside the city, the 
main spot is the Keukenhof tulip fields, 40 minutes 
by car. Helicopter flyovers can be arranged.
105:54:
AN AFTERNOON AT THE 
WALDORF ASTORIA
105:55:
A well-spent day passes through the Waldorf 
Astoria, housed in six 17th- and 18th-century 
palaces — gorgeous — facing the Herengracht canal. 
Start by pampering yourself at Spa Guerlain, the 
only one in the Benelux. On my last visit to the 
city, I enjoyed a foot soak followed 
by a wonderful relaxing massage, 
but you may opt for a myriad of 
treatments before heading off to enjoy the pool 
in the courtyard. Afterwards, it's "a must" to head to 
Peacock Alley, a room with sofas, wonderful 
for socializing, where afternoon tea is done with 
exceptional care (there's a photo of me with 
it on page 11, if you want a peek). Mine 
followed the theme of the moment — tulips — but 
there's another with miniature replicas of Dutch 
paintings. Both are a delight. And it's worth knowing 
that the gastronomic restaurant Spectrum has 
two Michelin stars. How about trying it 
on another day?
105:56:
WHERE TO STAY: All the hotels mentioned 
in this piece are excellent options.  
Alongside them, the brand-new Rosewood, which occupies 
a space that once housed the Palace of Justice, looks promising. 
On p. 212, read about De L’Europe.

Given that Taiko is a dinner recommendation, I couldn't 
think of a more delightful spot for 
lunch than Oriole (also open in the evening). Ideal 
for the most pleasant days, this delicious Mediterranean 
bistro takes over the hotel's interior garden and pairs 
with friends, conversation, cocktails, unhurried meals

tomato sauce and a serving of oysters on one side; 
pork chops with chorizo sauce and a portion 
of pata negra on the other. If you have the chance

of the 600-year-old building,

Between two canals, The Grand has

ART
The visual arts are in Amsterdam's DNA, and the city 
has a special place to concentrate some of 
its most beloved museums: the unmissable Museum-
plein. Around a lawn are gathered the Stede-
lijk, a reference in modern and contemporary art; 
the Moco, along the same lines but smaller and more provo-
cative; the Van Gogh, the most sensational among the mu-
seums dedicated to the Dutch master. Finally, the Rijks-
museum, guardian of the country's artistic spirit. Among 
iconic works by Johannes Vermeer, master of the Dutch Golden 
Age, you'll see dollhouses, a 
violin made of the typical white and blue Delft ceramic, 
many works of art and the famous canvas The Night 
Watch, by Rembrandt. Also Dutch, he refined 
the chiaroscuro technique created by the Italian Carava-
ggio and became one of the most important painters in the 
history of Western art. His house remains pre-
served and you can visit it at the Rembrandt House Mu-
seum, where his bed rests inside a wardrobe (a common 
custom among Europeans at the time) and his etching process 
continues to be taught to visitors.
106:24:
ANNE FRANK
A visit to the Anne Frank House is inevitable. For those who 
have read her diary, being in the same rooms she 
writes about, where she hid from the Nazis with 
family and friends until they were discovered (and her life 
ended in a concentration camp), is deeply 
moving. Seeing her small collections. Being reminded of 
her insights, striking for a girl. Don't 
miss noticing a pedestal with a large book. 
It names, one by one, the 102,000 Jews deported from 
the Netherlands during Nazism. "In spite of everything, I still 
believe in the goodness of humanity," she wrote.
106:25:
THROUGH THE CANALS
Want to know Amsterdam in detail? You need 
to see it from the canals' point of view, its ar-
teries. The design hotel Pulitzer, with its avant-garde and 
artsy vibe that matches the destination, organizes boat 
tours through them. Right in front of the hotel — which occupies 25 
typical 17th-century houses, each with its own style — 
board the Tourist, considered the oldest of the city's 
luxury pleasure boats, from 1909. Sea-
106:26:
ted where Winston Churchill once sat, learn 
from the guide that the little houses along the canal 
have reasons for their curious features. They're 
narrow because taxes were charged by the 
linear footage of the facade. They seem to lean 
forward because the attics jut out over the facades 
so grain could be stored. The hook under the 
roof? It was to hoist the sacks up. Sharpen your curiosity, 
keep the camera ready, and enjoy the ride.

HOTEL DE L’EUROPE
Close this magazine for a moment (but come back after!). On 
its back cover, you'll see a disruptive room in red 
that recalls Cildo Meireles's iconic work at Inhotim — but, in this one, 
you can stay. It's one 
of the new ‘T Huys suites, "The House" in old Dutch. 
A microcosm within the hotel signed by resi-
dent artists of the city, like the Janssen sisters, born in 
Curaçao, responsible for the suite in question.
107:36:
Anyone seeing this contemporary footprint would never guess 
we're talking about one of the city's oldest hotels. 
Located opposite the Mint Tower, where an entry fee 
was paid, all the merchant boats arriving once passed 
through it. Yes, boats — majesti-
cally, De L’Europe sits on the Amstel River. In the warmer months, the 
terrace alongside the brasserie Marie amplifies the charm. The hotel is 
a guardian of tradition, with a style — for the most part 
— classic and imposing.
107:37:
But, as we've seen, the new moves 
follow another path. The fully 
renovated restaurant Flore, two 
Michelin stars and a green one, echoes 
ultra-current, sustainable 
trends. Staying there, I had the op-
portunity to speak with chef Bas 
van Kranen the day after the reopening
107:38:
night. After showing me the room reserved for 
guests to meet the ingredients before dinner, 
he told me:
107:39:
"I don't call it being sustainable, I call it being attentive. Because 
an organic tomato grown in a greenhouse next door doesn't 
always consume less energy than one 
from Italy; but I want to be close to the producers 
because that way I can influence the processes from within. For 
example, at the fish farm that supplies us, we were 
able to introduce a Japanese technique of killing the 
animal that is humane, painless, but little applied 
because it is slower and, therefore, more expensive."
107:40:
The same with the decor — the wood for the tables 
comes from trees in the region that were felled 
by storms and the insulation 
is made with charcoal and hemp, an 
ancient technique that results in a very 
modern look. There's an omnivore menu 
and a botanical one — as they call it. De L’Europe keeps 
the legacy of the past, and casts its gaze on the 
future. Like Amsterdam. [LT]

Wishing for a celebration that lets you 
celebrate for longer than an ordinary ceremony 
allows? And, above all, wanting to live 
an experience YOUR WAY and together with your 
favorite people in the world?
114:6: The answer is a wedding on the beach.
114:7:
On the following pages, see our tips for making 
that happen and a fantastic selection of hotels 
to inspire your celebration trip. From now on, 
we wish you an “I Do” in beach chic style!

AS THE SOUNDTRACK TO YOUR WEDDING?  
THE BLUE OF SEA AND SKY AS YOUR BACKDROP?
