115:5:
I
Choose a place with hotels for 
different budgets, and with easy access. 
Even better if it has a 
special meaning for you (to share 
with the guests a
part of your story).
115:6:
II
Hire an organized team that 
makes your lives easier: a travel 
agency specialized in destination 
weddings, like PRIMETOUR, and a 
planner at the destination who knows 
the local vendors and logistics.
115:7:
III
Invite people well in advance. 
They need time to plan.
115:8:
IV 
Visit the city a few times to meet 
the professionals involved and the event 
spaces in person, besides 
doing the tasting sessions.
115:9:
V 
Share with the guests a website with  
all the possible tips about the place 
(restaurants, tours, hair salons).
115:10:
VI 
Leaving gifts in their rooms 
is a thoughtful idea (we suggest 
items that relate both to the 
couple and to the destination).
115:11:
VII
Let the guests feel free 
to enjoy the trip (in other words, 
don't create a schedule packed 
with activities).
115:12:
VIII 
But plan more than one 
day of celebration, so everyone 
can make the most of the chance  
to celebrate together.
115:13:
IX
Important: a rest day 
between the pre-wedding and the 
wedding itself. To recharge the 
energy before an unforgettable 
celebration!
115:14:
X
Trust the professionals and 
relax! The moment doesn't come back, and the 
job of the couple is to live it, not 
to worry about it.

Watch the windows: red flags bearing the image of the 
Baby Jesus are a charming tradition, still kept alive. 
A reminder of the strong Catholicism among the older generations 
of Portugal. Worth noting: Lisbon Cathedral, built in the 12th century, 
hosts a Midnight Mass within its sober walls.
129:13:
Elegant. The Christmas decoration bears 
no resemblance to the excesses 
of green and red, Santa Clauses, 
flashing colored lights of other 
parts of the world. The lights remain 
(mostly, at least) in 
white tones, hung in 
graceful decorations throughout the more 
historic neighborhoods. Among the unmissable 
illuminations — and the mild cold 
makes nighttime strolls a rather pleasant 
pursuit — 
are those of Armazéns do Chiado and 
Rua Garret, right in front, and 
Avenida da Liberdade. Praça 
do Comércio is also a focal 
point facing the Tagus for viewing the lights, 
with a great tree that every 
year is illuminated.
129:15:
In Lisbon, panettone does not reign. The time and place belong to the Bolo-Rei, also 
a dough with candied (and dried) fruit, but with another texture and 
shape: a crown (or a large doughnut). A tradition also in some 
parts of Brazil on the day celebrating the Three Kings — January 6 —, legend 
has it that it was born at the pagan feast of Saturnalia, in honor of the god 
Saturn, which took place in December (2,000 years ago). It was “fashionable” in the Middle 
Ages and had a revival in the France of Louis XIV. From this time comes the recipe 
that arrived in Portugal. If you want the country's most refined and creative (and, sometimes, 
controversial) version, there is nothing like the annual creations of pastry chef 
Francisco Siopa, from Penha Longa Resort, in Sintra.
129:16:
In Europe’s most agreeable winter, Lisbon is a less 
obvious idea — and, precisely for that reason, a 
delicious one — for a year-end trip. Beautiful Christmas 
lights, festive markets, skating rinks, themed parks. 
Cultural institutions dress up for the holidays — the 
renovated Ajuda Palace, for instance, often offers 
guided tours with actors dressed as King D. Luís I and 
Queen D. Maria Pia of Savoy, presenting a 19th-century 
Christmas. Follow us through a low-profile season as 
charming as few others!

In Lisbon there is also a São Silvestre race — but not on the 
31st, rather on the 27th. On the 31st the focus of Lisboners is on Praça do Comércio, 
which hosts a great popular celebration with concerts and fireworks. 
For those who must see the fireworks in the street, our suggestion is 
to seek out (slightly) calmer spots, such as the Miradouro da 
Graça, Parque das Nações or the Gardens of the Torre de Belém. For 
those who enjoy rituals, wearing white is not customary. But eating 12 
grapes at the stroke of midnight is essential!

WHERE TO STAY
There is a fantastic array of luxury hotels in the city. For the festive season, our pick is the Four Seasons Hotel 
Ritz Lisbon, whose themed décor is always a highlight. The hotels along Avenida da Liberdade, which is fully 
illuminated, are also great options. Among them, consider the Tivoli Avenida Liberdade and the Sofitel Lisbon Liberdade.  
There is always special lighting at Praça Camões, home to the Bairro Alto Hotel. In Sintra, the Valverde Palácio 
de Seteais deserves your attention.
